Department Newsletter

 

  • Reflections Volume VII

    E&ML ROCKSTARS

    WE MAKE ALL CAREERS POSSIBLE!

    FALL 2025

     
     
     

    GREETINGS FROM THE CHAIR!

    It’s hard to believe that I just finished my fourth year as chair of the department where I received my undergraduate degree 30 years ago and have worked for more than 20 years! It seems like just yesterday that I was illegally parking my blue Ford Tempo and running in to class. I had no idea that life would bring me full circle back to this place “...of wrath and tears,” (smile) and yes, it “Finds, and shall find me, unafraid.” These lines from William Ernest Henley’s famous poem “Invictus,” remind me as we celebrate Alabama A&M’s sesquicentennial that while we have accomplished so much, there is still more to do to continue this work. Certainly, “...my head is bloody but unbowed.” Though things get tough, as the largest general education base and academic department, we are up for the challenge of both teaching and learning to support and move this great enterprise forward, and I’m excited to lead this team of “ROCKSTARS” into the future!

    It’s hard to believe that I just finished my fourth year as chair of the department where I received my undergraduate degree 30 years ago and have worked for more than 20 years! It seems like just yesterday that I was illegally parking my blue Ford Tempo and running in to class. I had no idea that life would bring me full circle back to this place “...of wrath and tears,” (smile) and yes, it “Finds, and shall find me, unafraid.” These lines from William Ernest Henley’s famous poem “Invictus,” remind me as we celebrate Alabama A&M’s sesquicentennial that while we have accomplished so much, there is still more to do to continue this work. Certainly, “...my head is bloody but unbowed.” Though things get tough, as the largest general education base and academic department, we are up for the challenge of both teaching and learning to support and move this great enterprise forward, and I’m excited to lead this team of “ROCKSTARS” into the future!

     

    Dr. Charlotte Teague
    Chairperson

    AAMU Department of English & Modern Languages

    breaking news

     

    RetirEment

    SUSANNA VIZCARRA SAYS GOODBYE
    AAMU Spanish Instructor Retires

     

     

    Sigma Tau delta induction 2025

    E&ML ENGLISH HONOR SOCIETY

     

     

     

    Sigma Tau Delta is delighted to welcome our newest inductees: Marissa Colley, Madison McCain, Nadia Portis, and Charlene Yancey. Their dedication to literature, writing, and scholarship strengthens our chapter.

    We also wish to express our sincere gratitude to our outgoing board members: Taylor Hunter, President; J'Quez King, Vice President; Kennedi Pugh, Secretary; Sean Beach, Treasurer; Diamond Gooden, Public Relations Coordinator; and Nakeria Jenkins, Community Outreach. Thank you for your service and for modeling the spirit of excellence that defines Sigma Tau Delta.

    Congratulations to the newly elected board members: Marissa Colley, President; Madison McCain, Vice President; Kennedi Pugh, Secretary; Temi Taiwo, Treasurer, Diamond Gooden; Public Relations Coordinator; and Nadia Portis, Community Outreach. Members at-large are Nakeria Jenkins, Aaliyah Alexander, and Taylor Hunter. We look forward to the fresh perspectives and enthusiasm everyone will bring to our future events and service projects.

    A special thank-you goes out to our featured lecturer, Mr. Jacob Buechler, for his thought-provoking Last Lecture about postmodernism, identity, and hope. Looking ahead, Sigma Tau Delta is excited to announce plans for a Last Lecture series. Each semester, a faculty or staff member will be chosen by the student body to share the wisdom, experiences, and “final” advice they would offer if given the chance to deliver one culminating lecture.

    Contributed By: Ms. Deborah Karahalis

     

    • BANNED BOOKS 2025

      Banned Books 2025

      “CENSORSHIP IS SO 1984: READ FOR YOUR RIGHTS!”

      *The tree displayed in McCalep and McIntosh Hall was

      a conversation piece.

       

      Photos Courtesy of Dr. Kerry Brackett

       

      The title of this article is the theme for the 2025 Banned Books Week. Earlier this semester, the Top of the Hill Literacy Society met to discuss highlighting banned books and engaging students in conversation regarding literacy and censorship. To draw attention to this issue, committee members decorated trees with ornaments representing books prohibited by government officials, advocacy groups, and school boards. The “crime scene” tape used as garland was attention-grabbing and appropriate. Overall, the EML Department used Banned Book Week, October 5-11, 2025, to foster empowerment and illuminate literature!

       

      Contributed By: Pamela Martin

      READ BANNED BOOKS!!!

      What can you do? Take action and read a banned book today! Have a book talk or

      join a book club to promote and discuss banned books. For more information, go to: https://bannedbooksweek.org/

       

      Back to Top
    • HIGH SCHOOL SENIOR DAY 2025

      E&ML shines brightly!

      The EML Department was well represented at AAMU's High School, Community College, & Graduate Student Day on November 8. EML faculty and majors were on hand bright and early to tell prospective Bulldogs about the benefits of majoring in English at AAMU, providing information about major concentrations, classes, clubs, scholarships, The Write Place, and more.

      Contributed By: Ms. Pamela Manasco

       

      Back to Top
    • CONFERENCING WITH COLLEAGUES

      conferencing with colleagues

      E &ML WELL REPRESENTED AT PCAS/ACAS
       

       

      The Department of English and Modern Languages was well represented at the Popular Culture in the South/American Culture in the South Conference, which took place in October 2025 at the Embassy Suites in Huntsville. Four EML faculty members presented at the conference: Dr. Tracey Gholston presented “’Who Asked for This’? Plastic Representation and the Limits of Diversity in Charmed (CW 2018-2022)” on a panel dedicated to Intersections of Representational Black Experiences in American Television Series along with Dr. Lynn Washington, who presented “Authenticity and Authorship: Lena Waithe’s The Chi and the Redefinition of Black Cultural Narratives”; Professor Linda-Raven Woods shared “A River Diverted and the Challenges of the Verse Novel” on a panel devoted to Creative Writing; and Dr. Jonathan Malcolm Lampley commemorated the Emerald Anniversary of a cult classic with “Triple Distilled Horror: Scream and Scream Again at 55” on a panel concerned with Horror: History & Genre. In addition, Dr. Lampley was presented with a pin recognizing his involvement with PCAS/ACAS for more than 20 years during the Conference reception and awards ceremony. Each of the panels was well attended, and the presentations by EML faculty were received with great interest and enthusiasm, inspiring many thoughtful questions and comments from the attendees.

      Organized in 1971, the Popular Culture Association in the South is a regional offshoot of the slightly older Popular Culture Association and has published the academic journal Studies in Popular Culture for nearly half a century; a companion journal, Studies in American Culture, has been published since the late 90s and stresses the interdisciplinary study of American culture in general. Among the other topics covered at the 2025 conference were Black Music in Film, Comic Book Fandom, Games & Gaming, Social Media, Gender Inclusion, and Library Censorship. Specific works included such films as Curse of the Undead, Friday the 13th Part VIII: Jason Takes Manhattan, Sinners, and The Substance; TV shows such as Bob’s Burgers, The Great British Bake Off, and The Leftovers; and literary works such as As I Lay Dying, The Crying of Lot 49, Interview with the Vampire, Other Voices, Other Rooms, and A Streetcar Named Desire.

      For the first time in its 54-year history, the Popular Culture Association in the South chose Huntsville as the host city for the conference, and there is every indication that the Rocket City will be added to the rotation for hosting PCAS/ACAS again in the future, where it is hoped even more scholars from Alabama A & M University will share their research, insights, and creative endeavors.

       

      Contributed By: Dr. Jonathan Lampley

      Back to Top
    • SCENES FROM SAMLA

      Scenes from SAMLA

      E&ML Faculty 2025 Atlanta

      The South Atlantic Modern Language Association conference convened during November 6-8 at the Wyndham Atlanta Buckhead Hotel and Conference Center in Atlanta, Georgia. SAMLA welcomed 782 scholars from around the world for a time of intellectual sharing. Our English & Modern Languages department had delegation consisting of Dr. Charlotte Teague, Dr. Phillip Blackmon, Dr. Tracey Gholston, Dr. Lynn Washington, and Ms. Gentry Simmons.

      Highlights of the conference include the Thursday night featured speaker, Anthony Grooms, author of the award-winning novel Bombingham and the roundtable discussion (affectionately called The Black Girl Magic panel) that Dr. Charlotte Teague was a panelist on. The official title was, “Students’ Rights to Their Own Language: How’s That Workin’ for You?” and was chaired by Dr. Kendra N. B. Aya of North Carolina A&T. The women shared insights that encapsulated the work we do in the classroom while uniquely addressing the needs of students who attend HBCUs. Dr. Teague’s presentation on this panel was entitled, “Won’t He Do It? Yes, He will. Reinventing the HBCU Composition Classroom: Black Church Rhetoric as ‘Way Maker’”. Her contribution to this panel included how culturally relevant pedagogy, like Black Church Rhetoric, has been a key component in engaging students in the classroom.

      Dr. Phillip Blackmon’s presentation entitled, “Generative AI & the Composition Classroom: What Are We So Afraid Of?”, was focused on rethinking the teaching of composition with an AI Component by embracing and modeling ethical uses. In that same AI panel, Gentry Simmons’ presentation, "The "Real AI": Authentic Intentionality as a Case for Student Engagement and Educator Self-Care," explored how student engagement and educator self-care are important components of encouraging authentic voice in our students work.

      Last but not least, “Echoes of Esu: Navigating the Crossroads of Sacred, Secular, and Supernatural in Ryan Coogler’s Sinners” by Lynn Washington, and “‘We Cousins’: The Interconnectedness of Ancestral Spirits in Ryan Coogler’s Sinners” by Tracey Gholston explored Cultural Intersections in Ryan Coogler’s Sinners in each of their presentations as the conference came to a close. All of the AAMU EML delegation enjoyed the city of Atlanta, making connections, and leaving with invitations and opportunities to continue sharing their research.

       

       

      Contributed By Ms. Gentry Simmons

      Back to Top

    • SENIOR SPOTLIGHT

      Senior spotlight

      Fresh from a pre-service semester observing seventh grade ELA at Monrovia Middle School for the fall semester, senior Taylor Hunter feels prepared to tackle life after undergrad. A Memphis, TN, native, Taylor plans to return home after she graduates in May to teach eighth grade ELA. Taylor’s journey to Alabama A&M University began with a visit to campus, one that was the right size, the right distance from Memphis, and, as she explained, “It felt like I was home.” Taylor has been the president of the English Club for two years, is a member of Sigma Tau Delta, and Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Inc. As she reflected on her four years as an English major, Taylor said that some of her best memories come from the classes that she had with her fellow majors. “There was never a dull moment, even though most of us had several classes with each other," Taylor said, “The English professors all bring something different–and they are all characters.” She continued, “The classes weren’t boring, and I always felt free in the classes in a way that I don’t in others. The faculty encouraged our creativity and critical thinking.” For Taylor, even the classes that she had not planned on enjoying turned out to be positive experiences. “I don’t like horror movies at all, but Mr. B’s Film and Culture class was one of my favorites,” she shared. “Mr. Buechler taught me to question everything, even him, and now I am in the habit of second-guessing everything.” As Taylor’s undergraduate career comes to an end, she is optimistic about the future, and she hopes to continue to remain close to the EML department. “I plan on coming back for homecoming to watch the English Club in the parade,” she said. “And I am really looking forward to being a part of the homecoming alumni breakfast!”

       

      Contributed By: Ms. Theresa Mcwilliams-Wessels

      Back to Top
    • ALUMNI PANEL & SCHOLARSHIP AWARDS

      alumni panel &

      scholarship awards

      Back to Top
    • HOMECOMING 2025

      homecoming 2025

      A PARADE OF MAJORS!

       

      Members of the English Club and their faculty advisors gathered at 6 AM on October 4 to march in the homecoming parade. The parade route was extended by a half mile to accommodate the large crowds that gathered for the university’s 150th anniversary. “I always look forward to being a part of the parade,” said English sophomore Alyssa Durm. The parade’s route was 1.8 miles in total and students and the advisors passed out candy and carried a brand new banner that spanned over six feet. “I trained for this,” shared co-advisor Ms. Hannah Thomas. “I have been lifting weights so I could hold the banner.” The English Club was met with cheers along the route, with some onlookers shouting words of encouragement as they walked by. “What’s that sign say? The Department of English and Modern Languages? Happy homecoming, English Club!” was heard dozens of times throughout the morning from excited members of the crowd. “I’m just so happy that I was able to do this my senior year,” English Club president Taylor Hunter said. Co-advisor Ms. Stacey Humes added, “Honestly, waking up at 5 AM for the parade was the hardest part for me. Once I arrived and saw all the people –participants and spectators–all of my tiredness disappeared and my Bulldog pride took over!”

      Contributed By: Ms. Theresa Mcwilliams-Wessels

      Back to Top
    • THE WRITE PLACE

      The Write place

      FEATURE
       
      SPOTLIGHTING AAMU'S LOVING CENTER

      AAMU’s The Write Place

      SPOTLIGHTING AAMU'S LOVING CENTER CONTINUED

      Every college or university seems to have a writing center these days—you know, those places where you can go to get help on that term paper you were supposed to start six weeks ago and, oh yes, now it’s due tomorrow and what are you going to do? University writing centers are typically those go-to places that will help you brainstorm ideas, where you can get tutoring to brush up on rusty grammar skills, and where you can get tips to keep moving when you’re just plain stuck and the well has run dry.

      But how many universities can claim to have a Loving Center? Allow me to explain.

      No, that is not a new name to supplant AAMU’s The Write Place. And, yes, The Write Place will help you with all of the above. But The Write Place has evolved into so much more.

      When I first started teaching at AAMU, The Writing Center as we called it then was a cramped little facility hidden behind the old ROTC building, a full walk across campus for most students. Over time, the center was moved to its present location in 125 Carver where it has become a lively and student-centered hub for students from all disciplines.

      In the Fall semester of 2025, The Write Place has continued its mission as much more than just a place where students can come to get help on writing projects. It is a place where the written word is celebrated and brings people together. The Write Place kicked off the Fall semester with its Pages and Peace series. These workshops—held at the beginning, midterms, and at the end of the semester (when life is most stressful for both students and faculty!) provide an opportunity for relaxation and peace of mind through guided, meditative journaling.

      On September 30th, the College of Agricultural, Life and Natural Sciences hosted a panel discussion featuring conversations with alumni. October 2nd saw the arrival of Poetry on the Yard, a special reading featuring the work of Elizabeth Grigg, author of The Old ‘Normal Spirit.’

      On November 14th, a panel discussion with the College of Engineering, Technology, and Physical Science featured alumni and provided opportunities for students to engage with questions and discussions. Events such as these highlight The Write Place’s mission as an interdisciplinary hub across all colleges on the AAMU campus, underscoring a truth that has been too often neglected: Writing matters, regardless of major. And on November 19th, The Write Place graciously provided the facility for the hosting of the return of Writer’s Harvest, an annual event sponsored by The Department of English and Modern Languages that showcases student and faculty writing and helps to raise funds for The Food Bank of North Alabama.

      While these events are all great, what I would love to stress most about The Write Place is what it has become under the guidance of Ms. Kiietti Walker-Parker and Ms. Cerdrecia Stanton. I urge you to drop by on any given day, but especially on those days when you are feeling at the end of your tether, when you don’t feel like your nerves can handle one more pressing deadline. 125 Carver is like a little oasis, where the first thing you will see are the warm, inviting colors, welcoming smiles, relaxing music that will make you chill, shelves filled with favorite book titles, sometimes even snacks to give you that little pick-me-up.

      All of these little touches are why I say AAMU’s The Write Place isn’t just a writing center, but a loving center—a place that nurtures the soul as well as the mind.

        

      LEADERS READ AND WRITE!

      Contributed By: Ms. Linda Woods

      Back to Top

      Pant, Deepak. Participant. AI in Education: Building a World that Never Stops Learning. Pearson: 2025 Global EdTech Summit, 16 Oct. 2025. Webinar.

      Washington, Lynn. Chair. Through the Rivers: Reimagining Power and Possibility in Postcolonial and Diasporic Texts. 8 November 2025, South Atlantic Modern Language Association, Atlanta, GA.

      Stanley, Sheri. Participant. STEM Summer Institute Instructional Technology Integration. Alabama A&M University (June 2025)

      Vizcarra, Susana. Proyecto El Costurero de la Mistral: Poetry and Art, virtual exhibition launches 12/10/25.

    Back to Top
  • Reflections Volume VI 

     Reflections

    AAMU Department of English & Modern Languages

    April 2025

    Welcome from the Chair!

    There is so much about spring that I love. I am particularly tickled to see my azaleas blooming and my grass freshly cut for the first time. Things all around us are blossoming and that goes for the department as well! We're blossoming with ideas, activities, events, majors, new initiatives, our clubs, and hope for the future. This spring our work has been outstanding, and our reach has been far and wide, starting with the large (18 plus faculty & student) delegation that represented the department at ACETA at the University of Alabama in Huntsville! We had a faculty panel, a student panel, and many excellent faculty presentations. In addition, our Creative Writing Series has been first-rate, and we're excited about our new creative writing journal: The 1875 Review! Initiatives connected to our pre-law track are underway, and we offered our first course in that track this semester: The Novel (Literature & Social Justice) taught by Dr. Lynn Washington! The second major course will be offered this fall: Black Film (Social Justice in America). Additionally, our French and Spanish Clubs are thriving, our Awards Day celebrating our exceptional students was delightful, and certainly our biggest blossoming was celebrating our name change from English and Foreign Languages to English and Modern Languages! Our name change and ribbon cutting event was AMAZING, and marks a significant milestone in the department's history. There is no doubt that we are poised for greatness because certainly as indicated by this spring’s department slogan: E&ML ROCKS!
     

    Dr. Charlotte Teague

     

    In this newsletter:

    Department Events

    __________________

    Writing Center Update

    __________________

    Spotlights:

    Graduating Seniors & Major, Alum & Faculty

    __________________

    Club Updates

    __________________

    Faculty Highlights

    __________________

    Summer & Fall 2025 Preview

     

     

     
    Photo: Kiietti Walker-Parker
     
    A New Beginning
    By Alliyah Finley
     

    April 3, 2025 marked a new beginning for our department. On this day, we officially became the Department of English and Modern Languages. To commemorate our new name, we had a special ribbon-cutting ceremony that was filled with moments to remember. This event was a time of celebration, honor, and fellowship for faculty and students. Furthermore, it was a time for us to embrace our new milestone and the bright future that lies ahead.

     

    Many people attended our event, including Dean Dr. Peter Eley, Chair Dr. Charlotte Teague, current and former faculty, and English majors. At the start of the ceremony, we heard endearing remarks from Ms. Susanna Vizcarra, who shared why our occasion was significant. Dr. Teague then shared how we made it to this proud moment and how well our new name will represent us as a department. Soon after that splendid opening, we witnessed Dr. Teague, surrounded by faculty and students, gracefully cut the ribbon in front of our new name: The Department of English and Modern Languages. This title was displayed on a beautiful maroon sign which greets all who arrive to our building. Once the ribbon was cut, we spent the evening in joyous fellowship. During this time, our guests enjoyed music and cuisine from three distinct cultures: French, Spanish, and Chinese. The spreads included: brie, pastries, salsa, sesame chicken, vegetables, fruit, and various beverages.

     

    Our dessert was a delicious cake that was made for our beloved Dr. Jarrod Patterson, to celebrate his new beginning of retirement. For a few precious moments, we gathered to hear Dr. Patterson reflect on his twenty-six years in education and express his anticipation for the new season ahead. As he spoke, he was surrounded by family and close friends, whom all joined in a toast to his farewell. This time was truly a heart-warming addition to a wonderful evening. We ate, we laughed, and we shared our excitement for the great things in store for our department. As we remember how far we’ve come, we look forward to the journey ahead. We’ll continue to teach, inspire, and show everyone that we make all careers possible, and we make dreams come true. To the Department of English and Modern Languages, congratulations!

     

     
     

     

     

    • Events @ EML: Retirement

      After 26 Years, Dr. Patterson to Retire

      By Kiietti Walker-Parker

      It was a shock yet a loving surprise to hear that Dr. Jarrod Patterson would announce his retirement from Alabama A&M University after 26 years of service to the University’s students, community, alumni, staff, faculty, and administration when the Department of English and Modern Languages revealed an undercover retirement celebration that indeed surprised Dr. Patterson. He was full of quiet and demure yet visible tears as he reminded EML that we are indeed a family. As an introvert to those closest to him while an extrovert to the world, Dr. Patterson reminded us the definition of the word family: We sometimes fuss, fight, laugh, rejoice, celebrate, lament, disagree, agree, talk about from the sidelines which is what family does.

      He continued to reflect, “I came here as the youngest faculty member and newest English major graduate from Oakwood College, now Oakwood University, and was welcomed into what I never expected or knew would be possible, a family. I have worked under five chairs, deans, and presidents, yet the constant has been this family.” And family indeed showed out: present were former faculty members Alfreda Handy-Sullivan, Vertricia Jefferson, and Dr. Thelma Townsend; former chair, Dr. Paula Barnes; former admin, Ms. Loretta Townsend; his own family of families: Mrs. Patterson, their three boys, his sister, his brother, his sister-in-law, his nephew, and his best friend; and the EML crew who have weathered the storm of Dr. Patterson’s laughter, quick and sarcastic wit, humor, endeavors of academic and scholastic achievement, encouragement of his students to start here and go anywhere, and strong sense of well-being, intelligence, inspiration, and confidence in being true to himself and all those around him. We will miss Dr. Patterson but know that he will not be far from EML. Dr. Patterson has vowed that although this is a partial closing of a chapter of his life as a professor, he is embarking on his next calling, that next chapter in a book possibly titled, The Life and Times of Jarrod Patterson from Introvert to Extrovert: Stars Created by the Most High Came a Calling and You, One of the Greats, Are Answering that Call, which all of us at EML cannot wait to read. Cheers and cake Dr. Jarrod Patterson for a job to EML well-done!

       

      Back to Top
    • Events @ EML: Awards Day

       

       

      Back to Top
    • Events @ EML: Literary Journal

      By Rose-Ellen Ugbinada


      This fall, the creative writing committee began production on a project that has been waiting for a while: the d

      epartment’s very own creative writing magazine. In the 90s, the department produced a creative writing publication called Pulse, but it was short-lived. In 2019, Mrs. Manasco pitched the idea about reviving the journal so students could have a place to publish their creative writing and gain experience with working with it.

      The journal’s name, The 1875 Review, hono

      rs AAMU’s history and the current 150th anniversary celebration. The journal is unique because it only accepts submissions from HBCU students. The journal opened for submissions from HBCU students in Alabama in September 2024, and Issue One launched on April 22nd with a release party featuring authors reading their work. In Issue One, work by AAMU faculty and students will be featured. After this first issue, submissions will be open to HBCU students across the country

      .

      In the fall, students will have the opportunity to become editors, readers, and contribute decisions on whether a work will be published. Submissions for issue two 

      will open in September and be available until January. The journal publishes poetry, fiction, nonfiction, plays, and artwork from undergraduate and graduate HBCU students. To read Issue One, please visit the journal website at this link:

      The 1875 Review

      Back to Top
    • Events @ EML: Creative Writing

      Gwendolyn Brooks Poetry Contest 

      By Rose-Ellen Ugbinada

      The Gwendolyn Brooks Poetry Contest happens every spring, and submissions are typically open from February to the beginning of April. This year, the winners were announced at the EML awards ceremony on April 15th, with cash prizes for the top 3 winners. Congratulations to the winners:

      1st place: Jonathan Perkins, “Not a Monolith”

      2nd place: Jayla Blanks, “I Will Never Be Enough”

      3rd place: Jeremiah McDow, “Orange Moon”

      Honorable Mention: Tirenioluwa David, "Take Off That Mask"

      Honorable Mention: Isaiah Taylor, “Tempus”

       

       

       

      Creative Writing Workshops

      By Zanya Scott

      Calling all writers to The Write Place! Creative writing workshops promote writing of all forms to the students of Alabama A&M University. Created by AAMU English Instructor Pamela Manasco, these workshops are formed to bring writing to all students of the AAMU campus and not just English majors.

      For the creative workshop for the month of March, the prompt was about journaling and self-reflection on the spring semester. The focus was to identify and express emotions on how the spring semester is coming to an end. The host, Mr. Tyler, shared different tips and information on the benefits of journaling and how it can reduce anxiety and stress, which is common in college students. Afterwards, the floor was opened for sharing where students were able to discuss their thoughts and goals towards the spring semester. So come on by! We hope to see you during the last Spring 2025 workshop on April 18!

      Back to Top
    • Events @ EML: Creative Writing Events

       

      Back to Top
    • Events @ EML: Top of the Hill Literary Society

      The Handmaid's Tale

      By Mackenzie Lemon

       

      Photo: Kiietti Walker-Parker

      The Department of English and Modern Languages hosted a Top of the Hill Literary Society meeting on Thursday, March 27 to discuss The Handmaid's Tale by Margaret Atwood, including what stood out to them and how some events that have taken place in the book are popping up in real life. Mr. Jacob Buechler led the talk. Attendees were able to share how they felt about the book and some of the challenges that the character has faced, including alpha male energy, women's rights, toxic masculinity, and everyday challenges that women face, including how not all girls are girls' girls.

      I had the opportunity to interview Dr. Lynn Washington about her thoughts on The Handmaid's Tale and how it correlates to our society. Dr. Washington stated that the book had a lot of different languages "to manipulate reality and strip individuality by reinforcing patriarchal structure by banning reading for women." Dr. Washington also stated that "Language equates to controlling thought and power." In the age of social media, a lot of alpha male energy content has caught the attention of young boys through influencers like Andrew Tate and the Fresh and Fit podcast, just to name a few: people who think women should praise the path men walk on and that women should take care of themselves, the house, kids, and husbands.

       

      The tradwife movement has also become popular among young women. Tradwife, or traditional housewife, is described as women who prioritize traditional gender roles, domestic life, and homemaking. TikTok influencers like Nara Smith, Estee Williams, and Hannah Neeleman made the Tradwife movement popular. These women show how they make food from scratch, and tell stories on why they became a tradwife, how they take care of their family, and how to tap into your feminism. When I asked Dr. Washington about why she chose The Handmaid’s Tale for The Top of the Hill Literary Society, she mentioned that it contained "deep discussions on themes like power, gender, resistance, and dystopian control."

      Back to Top
    • Events @ EML: Open House

      Events @ EML: Open House

      By Theresa Wessels

      Open House 2025 was a huge success, thanks to participation from faculty, staff, and students. who proudly talked up the department’s many classes, major tracks, clubs, and other opportunities to the excited high school seniors touring AAMU. We’re so glad we were able to meet so many new students, and we can’t wait to meet our new majors in the Fall of 2025!

      Back to Top
    • Events @ EML: Scholarship

      Events @ EML: Scholarship

      English Pre-Law Major Receives Candy Girl Scholarship

      By Dr. Sheri Stanley

      Zoe Daniels-Sankey, an English-Pre-Law major, received a $500 scholarship during the Candy Girls Chronicles Scholarship Ceremony on April 8.

       

      The scholarship was made possible through proceeds from Candy Girls Chronicles, a book edited by department alumna Angela L. Dixon. The collection, curated by Dixon, features memoirs spanning 41 years from the fangirls of the ’80s singing group New Edition. The anthology explores themes of nostalgia, identity, and Black womanhood through the lens of fandom. Proceeds from the book funded four $500 scholarships awarded during the ceremony.

       

      Dixon remarked during the ceremony that “scholarships should come from unexpected places, and so should inspiration. I want students to know their stories matter—and that their education can take them further than they ever imagined.” She praised all of the recipients for their dynamic applications and commitment to academic excellence.

       

      Daniels-Sankey is a freshman Presidential Scholar from Illinois. She has a 3.8 GPA.

       

      Back to Top
    • EML Course Spotlight:ENG 307

      AAMU Brings Shakespeare to Life

      By Sean Beach

      With over 14 years of experience in teaching at AAMU, Dr. Powell brings a wealth of knowledge to the classroom. Dr. Powell began as a journalism major but quickly realized that journalism wasn’t his true calling. "I was inspired during a literature class taught by a professor who was equally liked and disliked by both teachers and students, but his methods stuck with me."

       

      This inspired him to teach, but Dr. Powell is not just any teacher; he sets himself apart through his drive to help students grow. He taught at other institutions, but has learned "the students here at A&M are different. Many come in less prepared, less motivated, and it’s rewarding to help bring them from a lower point to a higher point."

       

      Dr. Powell’s Shakespeare class covers seven plays throughout the semester, which allows students the opportunity to be able to dive into the themes, culture, and values of Shakespeare.

      Dr. Powell encourages freedom and creativity. "Students are freely engaged. I let them have some say in how they interact with the material. It’s not about strict structure, it’s about connection."

       

      I asked Dr. Powell why he believes Shakespeare is still relevant today. He stated, "He’s one of the greatest playwrights in history. His work shapes our understanding of human nature, values, and society. Having some level of proficiency in Shakespeare helps make students more well rounded, not just in literature, but culturally and socially." Dr. Powell's goal is to ensure that the name of Shakespeare is uplifted and that our students are able to be exposed to his works without having the idea that Shakespeare is "boring." Dr. Powell believes that Shakespeare isn’t just for English majors. "Students across all disciplines can benefit from being exposed to his works. Even if it’s not through a traditional class, exploring Shakespeare in different forms can enhance a person’s academic and personal development."

       

      Back to Top
    • EML Course Spotlight: ENG 308

      EML Course Spotlight: ENG 308

      Analyze, Interpret, Evaluate

      By J’quez King

      Dr. Charlotte C. Teague is an associate professor and Chair of English & Modern Languages at Alabama Agricultural & Mechanical University, where she specializes in professional writing, Black Women Writers, and Protest Literature. Dr. Teague earned both her bachelor's and master's degrees here at AAMU, and additionally earned another master's from the University of Alabama in Huntsville. She earned her PhD in English from Morgan State University. While obtaining her doctoral degree, she completed her dissertation on Black Motherhood and used African American Criticism to examine the literature. She began her career at Alabama A&M University in 2000 as a part-time faculty member and has taught English at Alabama A&M University for more than 20 years.

       

      Dr. Teague is currently teaching ENG 308: Literary Criticism, where her students learn to analyze, interpret, and evaluate literature. One of Dr. Teague's favorite literary criticisms is African American criticism, because it sheds light on issues that connect to us personally and helps us to identify and engage within the black experience. In class, Dr. Teague gains insights from her scholars who are challenged to use our literary criticism in real world situations. She loves the interactive discussions from the students and how diverse the class is, ranging from age, ethnicity and color. The class is filled with students from diverse backgrounds, allowing fair discussions from both men and women so we can gain a better understanding and offer equal input on the topics at hand.

      Dr. Teague inspires her students to learn our literary criticisms so we can apply them to our everyday lives. In the words of Dr. Charlotte Teague, "Do something today that your future self would thank you for." She invests time in her students, encouraging them to keep pushing on despite any obstacles they may face. In her parting words she wants to let all students know Don't Give Up and always keep your eyes on the prize.

      Photo: J’Quez King

       

      Back to Top
    • EML Course Spotlight: ENG 322

      EML Course Spotlight: ENG 322

      Digital Storytelling

      By Zanya Scott 

      Created by Alabama A&M University English Instructor Pamela Manasco, ENG 322: Writing for Digital Media is a class that teaches digital writing, such as creating and posting for social media accounts, making websites, blogging, writing news articles and creative nonfiction essays, and so much more. Writing for Digital Media is a great introduction into digital media spaces and proper internet etiquette. ENG 322 is also beneficial to any student with a desire to pursue a career in digital media spaces. Over the course of the semester, students have the opportunity to try their hands in different creative mediums while building an intricate portfolio.

       

      The last assignment in the class is the multimodal storytelling project. Students get to choose their own story to tell, and they present the final project in a multimodal format. This semester, English majors’ projects include Sean Beach’s photo essay about growing up in Washington, DC; Danny Coley’s multimedia video essay incorporating his original music; Ka’yla Dent’s TikTok series about poetry and self care; Natahniel Israel’s podcast about male leadership at AAMU; J’Quez King’s photo essay about tips for first generation college students; Temi Taiwo’s TikTok series about learning more about her Nigerian heritage and trying trying traditional foods; and Rose-Ellen Ugbinada’s interactive fiction game exploring alternate endings of Romeo and Juliet.

       

      ENG 322 is offered every other spring semester. Students will next have a chance to register in Spring 2027. We hope to see you in class!

      Photo: Zanya Scott

      Back to Top
    • EML Course Spotlight: ENG 326

      EML Course Spotlight: ENG 326

      Choosing Diversity in African American Literature II

      By Temi Taiwo

      Have you ever questioned what you believe or how you identify in terms of African American Literature? Dr. Tracey Gholston, who started teaching at Alabama A&M in 2017, teaches courses on African American literature that could provide the answers you need. Survey of African American Literature II is a part of a sequence that can be taken out of order. This class starts from the end of the Harlem Renaissance, 1935, to the present day. The class teaches students to interrogate who they are and what they believe in. The first statement Dr. Gholston gives her students at the start of the semester is, "We are going to find out what we believe and how we identify."

       

      Dr. Gholston’s incorporates essays and poems by Black African American authors, such as James Baldwin, Toni Morrison, Zora Neale Hurston, and Richard Wright. Students are reading across the spectrum, but only texts by Black authors in America. Although the class does not go over authors outside of America, Dr. Gholston can still teach about Jamaica Kincaid, who removed herself from St. John’s, Antigua to places like New York and Vermont. Before it was called Survey of African American Literature, the class was known as Black Literature where the class consisted of many writers from the black diaspora.

       

      Dr. Gholston also assigns a fun project for her students at the end of the semester where they can trace their lineage. This project was inspired by a national project called GirlTrek. Students create a digital project about who their community is and who helped them become the person they are today. This must be digital, either a picture collage or PowerPoint. Doing projects like this can help students learn more about themselves and the value of diversity.

      Photo: Temi Taiwo

      Back to Top
    • EML Course Spotlight: ENG 392

      EML Course Spotlight: ENG 392

      Social Justice in American Lit

      By Ka’yla Dent-Jones

      Social justice in American literature is the main topic of ENG 392: Special Topics in the Modern American Novel, which is being taught this spring semester. The course looks at the connections between American society's social justice concerns and literature. Students will examine how writers handle important issues including race, gender, class, and identity by studying important literary works. Students will get a greater awareness of the difficulties marginalized communities experience by investigating a variety of tales and viewpoints. They will also consider how literature influences public opinion and promotes social advancement. The ultimate goal of this course is to encourage students to think critically about the world and recognize how literature can promote knowledge and equality.

      Photo: Dr. Lynn Washington

      ENG 392 is taught by Dr. Lynn Washington. Dr. Washington is from Atlanta, Georgia, and she holds a B.A. and M.A. in English from Jackson State University, as well as a Ph.D. in English from Morgan State University. Her unwavering curiosity is one of her best qualities as a scholar and professor. "My research and teaching are influenced by my strong desire to learn and to explore new concepts. I like to support students in exploring their own intellectual interests, and I create classes that encourage development and participation. I make sure students can grasp the material and expand on what they already know." She also takes pride in her sincere concern for the achievement of her students. "I create an open and collaborative classroom environment where learning is a shared experience, and I am eager to learn from my students just as much as they learn from me." Dr. Washington seeks to establish a welcoming, stimulating environment where each student feels appreciated and heard.

      ENG 326 will next be offered in Spring 2026,

      and ENG 307, 308, 322, and 392 will next be offered

      in Spring 2027.

      Contact your advisor to learn more!

      Back to Top

    EML Updates: New Faculty

      
    Meet Ms. Stanton: The New Vision Behind AAMU’s The Write Place

    By Sean Beach

    Photo: Cerdricia Stanton

    Alabama A&M University’s Writing Center is going through exciting transformations. At the heart of this project is the newly appointed Coordinator, Ms. Cerdricia Stanton. As a proud graduate of both Alabama Agricultural and Mechanical University (AAMU) and the University of Alabama in Huntsville (UAH), Ms. Stanton is giving back in a unique and creative way to AAMU as she brings her love for reading, writing, and youth development to enhance her role as our Writing Center Coordinator.

     

    Ms. Stanton’s commitment to education and her love of writing didn’t just begin now. When asked what inspired her to step into her role as the Writing Center Coordinator, she emphasized the mentorship and purpose that she has received.

    Ms. Stanton explained, "I want to be part of something bigger than myself. I want to make a difference." She also mentioned that Dr. Teague’s encouragement over the years has been a true key motivator. One of Ms. Stanton’s goals is to rebrand the Writing Center as a university-wide resource. She has plans to expand the support that is given to our students in all majors and wants students to understand the importance of reading and writing. “Writing Across the Curriculum is something I want to push," she said. "I do not want to idly stand by and watch the lives of our students fall short of greatness solely because they lack the skills of writing."

    She wants to introduce writing workshops and other opportunities related to reading, writing, and analyzing for AAMU’s students, regardless of their majors, especially as she prepares for the upcoming academic year. "A lot of students aren’t exposed to different types of writing, and they struggle because of it, but with stronger reading and writing skills, they can grow not just academically, but personally." Ms. Stanton believes that writing is a life skill that is beyond the academic journey. "Writing will never leave you. Whether it’s drafting a plan, sending an email, or preparing a report, writing is always part of the process."

     

     

    Graduating Seniors

    English Major Profile: J'Quez King

    By Rose-Ellen Ugbinada

    J’Quez King was born and raised in Mobile, Alabama, and graduatedfrom C.F. Vigor High School in 2020. Outside of his English major studies, J’Quez enjoys majorette style dancing, learning to cook, and loves his friends and family.

     

    When on the hunt for a school, it is key to find one that feels like home. That is exactly what J’Quez did. “I chose Alabama A&M University because it was home away from home the minute I first visited the campus,” says King. J’Quez first visited A&M’s campus in Spring of 2019. He loved the HBCU atmosphere and the sense of familiarity that the campus brought to him. “Once I saw the campus from the Louis Crews Stadium I instantly fell in love.”

    J’Quez was first inspired to become an English major after taking Survey of American Literature with Dr. Tracey Gholston. “Her love for teaching English really inspired me to become an English major,” he says. “It was so much more engaging and interactive than my other classes. I advise all students to challenge themselves and become English majors.” Aside from the lovely Dr. Gholston, J’Quez has found several other English faculty members to be both inspiring and motivating. They have “inspired, pushed and motivated me to be the best at what I do and always made me strive for excellence inside the classroom. I am very grateful and appreciative of these faculty members for always believing in me as a person.”

    Aside from playing a tremendous role in our English department, J’Quez is involved in several clubs and organizations both in and outside of AAMU. J’Quez is the Founder of Common Grounds, an LGBTQ+ Advocacy group, as well as a member of Sigma Tau Delta English Honor Society. He is also involved in the Bulldog Elite Stomp and Shake Team, the AAMU Pep Squad, and the Bulldog Educator Association, and he is a member of Sigma Tau Epsilon Professional Fraternity Incorporated. J’Quez is also currently serving as the Chairman of the National Social Action and Special Projects Committee of Men of D.I.S.T.I.N.C. Incorporated, an LGBTQ+ Community Service based organization. He is the 2nd National Vice President Elect and will take office in July at their OASIS Convention. J’Quez plans to pursue a Masters in Secondary Education and Doctorate in Higher Education/Leadership, and aims to step into a secondary education teaching position focusing on teaching English at the High school level.

     

    Alumni Profile: Ke’Maria Jones

    By Mackenzie Lemon

    Ke’Maria Jones graduated from Alabama A&M University in May 2024 with a degree in English and a concentration in Secondary Education. “My professors really emphasized explaining the ‘why’ behind what we read and write. That mindset has helped me teach my students how to not only understand content, but explain it to others.”

     

    This year, she even wrote the script for her school’s Black History Month play. “It was a chance to use my writing skills in a creative way outside the classroom. I know I couldn’t have done it without being exposed to different writing styles and taking Dr. B’s Advanced Grammar course—it truly prepared me.”

     

    When asked about her favorite part of teaching, Ke’Maria shared, “I love helping my students understand plot elements—it’s exciting to watch them start to truly comprehend what they’re reading.”

     

    When reflecting on her time at AAMU, she highlighted two professors who made a big impact: Mrs. Wessels and Dr. Teague. “They chose the most engaging texts, which showed me how important it is for students to connect with what they read.”

        

    Her advice for current English majors? “Lean on each other and build strong relationships with your professors. And don’t give up—being an English major is so rewarding, and the career paths are endless!”

     

    Teague Selected as Rutgers Center Prestigious Program Fellow

     

    Dr. Charlotte Teague, associate professor and chairperson, has been selected to participate in the very competitive Rutgers Center for Minority Serving Institutions (CMSI) Aspiring Leaders fellowship program. The CMSI program is designed to develop the next generation of leaders at Minority Serving Institutions (MSIs) by providing them with the knowledge and skills necessary for success in higher education administration. The CMSI Aspiring Leaders program is a two-year initiative that connects aspiring leaders at MSIs with experienced mentors. The program covers a range of topics critical to successful higher education leadership, including fundraising, strategic planning, and crisis management. The program is led by Dr. MaryBeth Gasman, the Samuel DeWitt Proctor Endowed Chair in Education, a Distinguished Professor, and the Associate Dean for Research in the Graduate School of Education at Rutgers University, who is widely recognized as the leading scholar on Historically Black Colleges and Universities (HBCUs) in the country.

    EML Faculty Earn ACUE Credentials    

    Congratulations to Dr. Sheri Stanley, Dr. Charlotte Teague, and Dr. Yolanda Toney for earning ACUE Badges this spring for completing the following micro credential: Creating an Inclusive and Supportive Learning Environment!

     

    Faculty on the Move

    By Pamela Manasco

    In March, Dr. Charlotte Teague and Mrs. Pamela Manasco were able to travel to Los Angeles to attend the creative writing conference held by the Association of Writers & Writing Programs, thanks to generous grants. Dr. Teague won a scholarship from AWP’s HBCU MFA Think Tank, a program established to bring HBCU faculty and administrators together to discuss the logistics of starting the first ever MFA program at an HBCU. Mrs. Manasco won a 2024 poetry fellowship from the Alabama State Council on the Arts.

     

    As part of the HBCU MFA Think Tank, Dr. Teague met with creative writing faculty from North Carolina A&T University, Morgan State University, Howard University, and many others, led by committee members Regina Brooks, Kwame Dawes, and A. J. Verdelle. Dr. Teague was also invited to attend the AWP HBCU Fellowship Program reception, held at the J. W. Marriott Hotel. A key highlight of the conference for Dr. Teague was Roxane Gay’s keynote speech, where Gay proclaimed that “the pen is not mightier than the sword: the pen is the sword.”

    Mrs. Manasco attended several panels relating to poetry and publishing, including “Hell-Bent Hope: Poets Holding Fast During This Time of Ecological Crisis,” “Theories of Vastness: On Capaciousness in Poetry,” and “The Southern Lit Journal as Resistance.” She was also an invited guest at the Writer to Writer Mentorship Program Reception, having been a mentee in Summer 2023. Her highlight of the conference was hearing poet Camille Dungy discuss her writing practice.

     

    EML Club Updates

     

    English Club

     

    By Temi Taiwo

     

    Bonding with friends demonstrates a remedy to stress. Midterms can prove stressful especially when it comes to studying and planning for multiple classes. The time after is when students start to relax a little and try to find downtime. The English Club did just that on March 5 by having a bonding event for their members. Both the President and Vice President of this club, Taylor Hunter, and Kennedi Pugh, decided that it would be a great idea for the members to get together and learn more about each other. During this event, members watched One of Them Days starring Keke Palmer and SZA and played Get The Ick.

     

    Learning more about one another helps everyone decide other events that can be created to gain more members and for the enjoyment of everyone. Although only members attended the bonding event and not advisors, Ms. Thomas, Ms. Wessels, and Dr. Powell are making sure English Club is student-centered and led. When they attend the club’s events, it is to show their support for student endeavors. Dr. Powell adds that, "We want students to take as much active participation as possible, so we’re there more in a capacity of support than actually running."

     

    Creating the bonding event was also a way for everyone to get together and have a safe space and would have been an event outside of writing, because at the end of the day college students need some fun. Kennedi mentions this saying, "We wanted to show y'all we’re more than just an English Club doing mostly English based stuff."

    French Club

     

    By Danny Coley

    Photo: Deb Karahalis

    I was able to catch up with A&M’s very own Professor Brittany Massey to ask her the questions students have burning in their minds.

     

    How long has Alabama A&M had a French Club?

     

    “I think this is the third year. We originally started the club when we came back from COVID, but of course we didn’t do much because of COVID. So, it’s been about three years now.”

     

    What’s the difference between a crepe and a pancake?

     

    “A pancake is mostly flour, and because of that, it is denser. A crepe is VERY thin. And the main ingredient is eggs. That makes a crepe much more consistent.”

     

    Is there ever any intercollegiate meeting with other French Clubs?

     

    “We actually collaborate with some of the other HBCUs in the area, especially Oakwood right here in Huntsville.”

     

    How often in a month or semester does French Club meet?

     

    “Three times a semester. Nearly monthly.”

     

    What kinds of activities do you all do?

     

    “We try to make it cultural. More culture, less classroom. We sometimes put on a film night. This year we wrote valentines cards. Crepe day where we make crepes. Game day (guess who. Jenga.)”

    Poetry Club

     

    By Ka’yla Dent-Jones

    During Women’s History Month, AAMU’s Poetry Club, The Society of Lyrically Advanced Poets, encouraged all Alabama A&M students to attend their upcoming event on March 20th, 2025, at 7:00 pm at The Foxhole Lounge. S.L.A.P has always encouraged students of different talents, not just poets, to join their outstanding organization and showcase their gifts.

     

    At The Foxhole Lounge, students and Huntsville residents supported S.L.A.P. Mister S.L.A.P, Kyron Fletcher, hosted the event and included the poetry club's very own “5 Minutes of Fire.” The participation from the members, students, and the people of Huntsville brought the event to life. The excitement and life of the talents shown that night were outstanding.

    S.L.AP. will also be celebrating Poetry Month in

    April with their 8 Annual Hands & Hearts Festival April 25 .

     

    Member Charles Boyce says, “I knew we were going to be a big family the moment I walked into the interest meeting in August. The fact that I was accepted as a complete stranger and that we shared poetry, art, and the overpowering realization that, despite our organization, we will still have fun struck a chord with me. I joined a poetry club because of the variety of poems written by poets of diverse

    backgrounds and styles; you can always learn something new from other members, and I feel like I can truly develop my poetic voice by reading the works of other amazing poets my own age.”

     

     
    English Pre-Law Club
    Pre-Law Club Hosts Alum for Inspiring Talk

    By Kennedi Pugh, English Pre-Law Major

    and Dr. Sheri Stanley, Pre-Law Club Advisor

    The English-Pre-Law Club hosted Angela L. Dixon, an alumna of the English and Modern Languages Department, for an inspiring Purdue University- Global HBCU Technical Talk on Tuesday, April 8, in the Writing Center. This event was designed to encourage undergraduate students to pursue graduate education and explore interdisciplinary career paths. A multi-talented attorney, professor, author, speaker, mentor, and game show winner, Dixon brought her wealth of experience and passion for service to center stage.

     

    Dixon received a grant to deliver a technical talk aimed at galvanizing undergraduate students, especially those at HBCUs, to consider pursuing graduate degrees. Dixon highlighted the importance of strong writing, research, and critical thinking skills—core elements of an English degree—in preparing for a doctoral program. She also discussed her educational journey in detail, including her path to earning a law degree and entering the legal profession. “An English degree gave me more than a love for literature—it equipped me with the analytical tools, writing skills, and intellectual confidence I needed to succeed in both graduate school and the legal profession,” Dixon shared. “I want students, especially those at HBCUs, to see that their voices and talents belong in these spaces.”

     

    Originally from Mobile, Alabama, Dixon now resides in Houston, Texas. She earned her B.A. in English from Alabama A&M University in 1993, followed by an MBA from St. Ambrose University in 1998, and a Juris Doctor from the University of Iowa College of Law in 2003. With over 21 years of legal experience and more than 15 years of teaching at the collegiate level, she is currently pursuing a Doctor of Education in Leadership and Innovation at Purdue Global University. Additionally, she plans to join the EML adjunct staff to teach Introduction to English-Pre-Law in fall 2025.

    Photo: Dr. Sheri Stanley
    Sigma Tau Delta

    By J’Quez King

    Sigma Tau Delta English Honor Society strives to promote high achievement in English language and literature in undergraduate, graduate, and professional studies. The Society provides cultural stimulation on college campuses and promotes exemplary character and good fellowship among its members. AAMU houses the Beta Nu Chapter of Sigma Tau Delta, providing outstanding service, scholarships and internships for our majors. The chapter is led by President Taylor Hunter and Vice President J’Quez King.

    Over the spring semester, Sigma Tau Delta hosted various events for our community, including participating in Read Across America, where we read and donated a book to the childcare development center on the Alabama A&M campus. Read Across America is a yearly event Sigma Tau Delta participates in to pour into the young kids in childcare. “We thoroughly enjoy this occasion because it allows us to pour into the youth” says advisor Tracey Gholston.

    We also hosted our first Write to Womanhood Literature Tea honoring our women in literature. The tea was an original idea by Sigma Tau Delta member Kennede Boyd and carried out by member J’Quez King on Mar 25, 2025 in The Write Place. Sigma Tau Delta invited special guest panelist Ms. Bathsheba Johnson, who works at the EarlyWorks, Museum, and Dr. Yolanda Toney, an English instructor at Alabama A&M University. The event honoring women writers such as Zora Neale Hurston, Phillis Wheatley and Alice Walker was the highlight of the tea.

     

     
    Spanish Club

    By Sean Beach

     

     

    At Alabama A&M University, Spanish Club is helping students make connections between culture, history, and community. Spanish Club is sponsored by two of our Spanish instructors, Ms. Susana Vizcarra and Dr. Koji Nishida. Spanish club is a hub for cultural enrichment, creativity, and learning.

    Ms. Vizcarra has been teaching Spanish at all levels for the past 20 years. She holds a bachelor’s degree and two master’s degrees from Texas State University and the University Alabama in Huntsville. "Learning a second language isn’t just about grammar and vocabulary. It’s also beneficial from a psychological point of view." Ms. Vizcarra believes in helping students develop a new level and perspective on culture. This will also help students become more confident and differentiate themselves in their professional journey.

    The Spanish Club gives students opportunities to experience the Spanish-speaking culture in very interactive ways. There are events like Spanish Bingo, which is hosted to provide a variety of activities which blends the learning with creative fun. "The club is a way to offer enriching experiences outside of class, students can connect through language, culture, and even art." Some of Ms. Vizcarra’s favorite events have included a workshop on the Carnival de Loíza that takes place in Puerto Rico. Students were able to learn about the fusion of African and Caribbean traditions. This also included mask painting, which allowed students the opportunity to create traditional carnival masks.

    As the club continues to grow, the future looks bright. With around 16 active members, the Spanish Club is becoming even more successful and impactful to our school community. 

     

    Faculty Highlights

    Compiled by Pamela Manasco

    Jody Jones

    Ms. Jones presented “Addressing Digital Literacy Challenges for First Year Students in the Composition Classroom” at the ACETA conference in February.

     

    Dr. Jonathan Lampley

    Dr. Lampley presented “Music of the Night: The Phantom of the Opera at 100” at the ACETA conference in February.

     

    Pamela Manasco

    Mrs. Manasco’s poetry publications this semester include “Little Bluestem” in Identity Theory, and “After Telling My Therapist the Anxiety is Worse” in RockPaperPoem. Additionally, two of her poems were nominated for Pushcart Prizes in December 2024. Her first full-length poetry manuscript was named as a finalist to The Louisville Review’s National Poetry Book Contest in February, and to Terrapin Books’ open call in March. In May, she will attend a week-long invited writer’s retreat at the Lowrey Guest House at Pebble Hill at Auburn University, and give an author’s talk.

     

    Dr. Robert Powell

    Dr. Powell presented “Barbie--You are not Kenough. The Only Box Restraining You is Your Own: Realistically Realizing and Not Utopianly Utilizing Female Power from the film Barbie” at the ACETA conference in February.

     

    Dr. Charlotte Teague

    Dr. Teague presented “Double Consciousness and the American Dream: Bigger, Walter Lee, and Clay-- Invisible Men with Deferred Dreams” and ““In the Protest Tradition: African American Women Writing in Power and Purpose” at the ACETA Conference in February. Dr. Teague also won a Rutgers University/Minority Serving Institutions (MSI) Aspiring Leaders Fellowship, as well as a scholarship from the Association of Writers & Writing Programs’ HBCU MFA Think Tank. Dr. Teague’s publications this semester include three book reviews for the Alabama Writers Forum: Terah Shelton Harris’s Long After We Are Gone, Mary Munroe’s Bent but Not Broken, and Angela Jackson-Brown’s Untethered. She also published the Encyclopedia of Alabama article “Yaa Gyasi.”

     

    Dr. Yolanda Toney

    Dr. Toney presented “Intimate Conversations about Friendships, Relationships, and Situationships” in the Small-Talk Concert Series at Brown Books and More in Decatur, Alabama. She also presented “Protest Literature: Making a Way out of No Way” at the ACETA Conference in February. Additionally, she led the talks “Truth Not Dare” at The Curated Gallery, and the “Write to Womanhood” panel discussion for Sigma Tau Delta English Honor Society at AAMU.

     

    Dr. Lynn Washington

    Dr. Washington presented “Identity as Protest in Modern American Literature” at the ACETA Conference in February.

     

    Linda-Raven Woods

    Mrs. Woods’s novel Elmer & Leonard was a Carol Houck Smith Fiction Fellowship finalist at the Wisconsin Institute for Creative Writing, and her book Drums, Guitars, Blues & Ghosts was a finalist for the Schaffner Press’s Nicholas Schaffner Award.

    Special thanks to our contributors . . .
